[124:40]I see people saying they should pass standalone bills, multiple bills, one bill with the tax cut, one bill with immigration, one bill with this and

[124:50]the reason why it has to be this way, it has everything to do with the Senate parliamentary rules.

[124:57]Everything, every major piece of legislation in the Senate requires a super majority to pass.

[125:04]So if you want an immigration bill, it's going to take 60 votes.

[125:08]Now, you think it's a simple majority, but the Senate has a rule.

[125:12]It's called the filibuster rule.

[125:14]And it says that any member can fill a buster legislation

[125:18]and prevent it from coming to the floor for a vote.

[125:21]The only way that you can forcibly end debate

[125:25]on legislation is by invoking cloture.

[125:28]That takes 60 votes.

[125:30]And so they call this the fill a buster rule.

[125:32]That's why everything requires 60 votes, not 51,

[125:36]but 60 votes to pass.

[125:40]Except for one special set of circumstances,

[125:44]it's called the budget reconciliation process.

[125:47]And we'll get into detail on what that means.

[125:50]But the reason why it has to be this way,

[125:52]why it's so controversial,

[125:54]why it's 10 pounds of shit in a five pound bag

[125:57]is because we need to use the budget reconciliation process

[126:00]to get anything passed.

[126:02]And in order to do that,

[126:04]we basically need a grand bargain.

[126:07]between the various wings of the Republican conference,

[126:10]and that is the Republican representatives

[126:12]in the House and the Senate

[126:15]to get what we want on immigration, that's why.

[126:19]So we'll talk about the bill,

[126:20]we'll talk about the process,

[126:22]how we've gotten here,

[126:23]because the bill was debated in the House,

[126:26]and it passed a few weeks ago by one vote,

[126:29]literally one vote.

[126:31]The Republicans have a very slim majority in the House,

[126:34]and it passed by one.

[126:35]It went into the Senate and after a lot of debate, eventually it passed the Senate, 50-50.

[126:43]The vote was a tie.

[126:45]It was 50-50.

[126:46]That was, I believe yesterday or the day before.

[126:49]And in the case of a tie vote, the vice president is the president of the Senate and he can cast

[126:55]a tie breaking vote.

[127:00]The Senate made modifications to it. It now has to go to the Republicans in the House to get

[127:05]cleared again. And if the Republicans pass it in its current form, it goes to the president's desk

[127:11]and it becomes law. If not, it has to go back to the Senate. If they make modifications, then it

[127:18]goes back to the Senate. And so this is where we are tonight. Like I said, as we speak, there is a

[127:29]We don't know the outcome of it

[127:30]at the time of doing the show right now.

[127:35]but we should know by the end of the night.

[127:36]So by the end of the night,

[127:37]we should be able to break the news

[130:16]They said I was pulling my punches and I'll talk about that

[130:35]and one person speaks and we engage with the ideas.

[130:39]I believe that he was intellectually honest.

[130:42]I think he engaged in good faith.

[130:44]I think he responded to my arguments

[130:45]and I responded to his.

[130:47]We were respectful, we kept it civil.

[130:50]We didn't interrupt each other.

[130:52]We were respectful of equal time.

[130:54]And that's all I've ever wanted.

[130:58]He said, I expected that you would come on the show

[131:03]because I'm Indian.

[131:37]I care a lot.

[131:38]I care a lot about the subject matter.

[131:40]I care a lot about my position.

[131:46]and I am a massive believer in having the discussion.

[131:53]when both people are able to talk

[131:55]and both people are acting in good faith,

[131:58]I think that our ideas win, and I don't even think it's close.

[134:54]This show America first and ever since then as you know, it has been an uphill battle and a struggle

[135:02]Not not to break into the industry not to make a name for myself or build an audience

[135:08]I've had no problem doing that. I've actually found that to be pretty easy

[135:12]The problem that I have always had is that I have been blackballed by the system

[135:19]I have been totally, and I'm not playing the victim here.

[135:22]I'm just telling it like it is.

[135:25]Ever since I started, because I voiced opinions

[135:29]that were outside the mainstream on race and on Israel

[135:33]and on Jews, for those reasons, I was blackballed.

[135:38]And there was a concerted effort

[135:40]from the time I was 18 years old.

[135:43]I can tell you the names,

[135:44]I could tell you what they did and how they did it.

[135:49]to prevent me from getting any kind of access.

[135:53]And by that, I mean, I was banned from attending CPAC.

[135:58]I was banned from having anything to do

[136:00]with Turning Point USA early on.

[136:03]I was banned from having anything to do

[136:05]with College Republicans, young Americans for Liberty.

[136:11]And when the national organization found out,

[136:13]they threatened to disband the chapters

[136:16]if they didn't disinvite me.

[136:18]was banned from the turning point events. I took a photograph with the turning

[136:24]point ambassador, she was removed from the ambassador program because of the

[136:28]photo. And publicly and privately they smeared me. There was a whisper campaign

[136:36]behind the scenes and first they said I was a racist. That's how it started in

[136:41]2017 through 2019.

[136:44]They said he's a real racist. He's a he's one of these white nationalists. He's a Holocaust denier. He's an anti-Semite

[136:51]Because at that time

[136:53]Everybody was pro gay pro immigration pro Israel

[137:04]So that was the whisper campaign.

[137:06]And then they took it public and they said,

[137:07]this guy, they would take my clips out of context

[137:10]and they'd say, this guy's a hateful racist,

[137:12]white supremacist.

[137:14]Then when the right wing moved to the right

[137:16]and they embraced all my positions,

[137:20]they embraced my criticisms of anti-white racism.

[137:23]They embraced my repudiation of the LGBT social revolution.

[137:29]They embraced my emphatic rejection of dual allegiance

[137:36]and all the rest of it, legal immigration.

[137:43]He's a divide and conquer federal agent.

[137:49]of my life and my career,

[137:51]The most difficult thing for me has been repairing this reputational damage and trying to gain

[137:59]access to the industry.

[138:01]I've done well on my own.

[138:03]I've built up a big show and a big following, and I've made a name for myself with zero

[138:07]institutional anything.

[138:16]But they won't let me through the door.

[138:18]They have gate kept me for the better part of a decade.

[138:30]your feelings are about him, without a doubt, he has been truly a fixture in the right wing

[138:36]for 25 years and maybe even longer than that.

[138:46]He's written dozens of books

[138:49]Produced some of the biggest conservative films one of the biggest pundits and so for him to agree to a debate

[138:57]This was a huge opportunity for me

[139:13]people that

[139:14]Only believe what they heard about me secondhand or they saw a clip or they don't like me for one reason or other

[139:20]this was an opportunity where they could see what I'm really about and

[139:26]So for that reason it turned into a big platform in my opinion

[139:30]He is a mainstay of mainstream conservatism

[139:34]You could say he's a standard bearer of a certain kind of conservative culture warrior

[139:40]from a different generation.

[139:41]And I think I represent a culture warrior from my generation.

[139:46]And I didn't get approved.

[139:48]I was never led in through the front door.

[139:55]in the era of Donald Trump.

[139:58]So, I think there was a profound significance there.

[140:01]And of course, we're debating one of these seminal issues, which is the Iran War.

[140:05]And to get into the actual content of the debate, like I said, I didn't really know

[140:11]He was a little bit nasty to me on Twitter.

[140:18]He basically said, Israel doesn't need me to die for them in their war because I'm

[140:31]Dinesh D'Souza is like an Indian nerd. I'm a white nerd. Okay. I don't think any of neither of us are

[140:38]are gonna be on the football team. Neither of us are gonna be

[140:44]taken down. You know what? I'm trying to keep a PG because it's the beginning of the show.

[140:51]I'm like, we're both dorks.

[140:53]So I didn't know if it was gonna be totally hostile or not

[140:55]because it kind of started a little hot on Twitter.

[140:58]And someone laid down the challenge.

[140:59]They said, you will never debate him.

[141:01]And he said, I'll debate him.

[141:03]He took the challenge and I said, okay,

[141:05]well let's set it up.

[141:06]So we set it up on Alex Jones

[141:08]and it turned out to be extremely academic and cordial.

[141:11]And we got into the war in Iran

[141:14]and I laid out, in my opinion, a very technical case.

[141:19]I laid out the case that I've made on my show.

[141:22]And the key thing that I sought to reframe

[141:25]about the conflict is this.

[141:28]Whether you agree with this or not,

[141:30]and I know most of you do not,

[141:32]but many of the American conservatives,

[141:35]you could call them boomers,

[141:36]you could call them mainstream conservatives,

[141:39]whatever you wanna say.

[141:41]Mainstream conservatives believe

[141:43]that going to war with Iran is a good thing.

[141:45]And you saw that.

[141:47]80 some percent of Republicans supported Trump's strike on Iran.

[141:51]That's what we're dealing with.

[141:53]And so I'm anticipating a little bit some of the criticism which I'll address.

[141:58]Some people said I didn't go hard enough.

[142:01]We have to start out and analyzing the debate by saying.

[142:05]This is the environment that we're in.

[142:08]Contrary to what people believe, you actually need to touch grass a little bit.

[142:12]Most of the Republicans are normies.

[142:14]Most of the Republicans are still brainwashed.

[142:17]They're still neocons.

[142:18]And many of them are very deep into the Trump cult.

[142:23]And I like Trump.

[142:25]I didn't vote for him.

[142:26]I have my reasons for that.

[142:28]But whatever he says is what they will support,

[142:31]whether it is true to America first or not.

[142:35]And sometimes that can be a good thing.

[142:37]Sometimes when Trump is pushing on an issue in the right way,

[142:41]he's leading the conservatives with him

[142:43]to the right direction.

[142:45]But when he's pushing in the wrong direction,

[142:47]like he's pushing us into a war with Iran,

[142:49]he's gonna take the conservatives with him

[142:51]and that's a bad thing.

[142:53]Sometimes he says, you know what, we're gonna have alligators eat illegal immigrants and

[142:57]his supporters say, hell yeah.

[142:59]And he go, this is great.

[143:00]This is a very positive effect of the Trump cult.

[143:04]No one else could persuade the base to support this.

[143:07]But then he says America first means bombing Iran.

[143:11]And then he say, hang on a second, maybe we have a problem here.

[143:14]And so 80 some, I think it was 86% of Republicans supported the strike on Iran.

[143:21]And this is because of a very diligent and concerted effort by the Israel lobby in the

[143:27]United States to make a case.

[143:29]And this is something that people on our side, you really need to listen to this and internalize

[143:36]this.

[143:37]I think it's clear.

[143:43]I'm, I think a good debater.

[143:46]I think I'm logical.

[143:47]I think I'm a reasonable person.

[143:50]So are they.

[143:52]So are our adversaries.

[143:55]The Israel lobby, the pro-Israel crowd, whether they be evangelical, Zionists or national

[144:00]conservatives or Jews themselves.

[144:03]They are also pretty skilled at persuasion.

[144:06]They've been doing it a long time.

[144:08]There's a reason that mainstream conservatives

[144:10]support Israel so much.

[144:12]It's because they are masters of the media.

[144:14]They are masters of manipulation.

[144:16]They're masters of marketing and persuasion.

[144:20]And if you've been paying attention,

[144:22]they have adapted with the times.

[144:26]They're not saying the same message they wore 10 years ago

[144:30]For some it may appear subtle. I don't think it is but for some it may appear subtle

[144:35]Maybe five to 10 years ago, conservatives would come out and say,

[144:40]Israel's our closest ally.

[144:42]We need to go to war to defend Israel because they're the only democracy in the Middle East.

[144:46]And the Muslim countries throw gays off of buildings and they don't let women drive.

[144:52]But Israel has gay pride parades and feminism.

[144:56]And they used to think that was persuasive and it was.

[145:01]If you paid attention, when Trump bombed Iran, they didn't say that.

[145:06]What did they say?

[145:08]They massaged the truth.

[145:10]They said, well, Trump always said he was bombing Iran's nuclear program.

[145:16]You're not against Trump, are you?

[145:19]This is what they said.

[145:21]They said, well, you know, technically Trump always said we were bombing Iran's nuclear program.

[145:26]They said, and Israel's doing most of the heavy lifting, so aren't they fighting our

[145:31]wars for us?

[145:33]They said, this isn't boots on the ground.

[145:35]This is not like Iraq.

[145:37]This is limited.

[145:38]This is narrow.

[145:39]We're gonna get in and get out cleanly.

[145:41]They said, we're not nation building.

[145:43]We're not even pursuing regime change.

[145:46]Remember, this is how they played it for two weeks.

[145:49]And they made a very compelling case because they learned.

[145:52]They knew it.

[145:53]They know that they're living in a world where Iraq.

[145:56]happened where Syria and Libya happened, and they have calibrated their message. They changed

[146:02]the message. And that's why, and look, it worked. 86% of Republicans supported Trump's

[146:10]strike on Iran. That's the vast majority. And if you look at Tucker Carlson's comment

[146:16]section or Candace Owens, they are more mainstream. They're bigger than me.

[146:21]And you have many of the comments are saying, you lost me.

[146:25]We're with Trump, we need to bomb Iran.

[146:28]This is a reality.

[146:30]And it's easy to get caught up in our section of the internet

[146:33]where we know the truth.

[146:35]You go on this show, it's like a bucket of ice water

[146:38]to borrow an expression.

[146:40]You go on this show and it is like you're stepping out of Plato's cave

[146:45]You're getting hit with the full broad daylight and you see the whole thing

[146:49]But we are in the minority of the minority. It's a section of a section of the right wing that is with us

[146:55]And so I went into this debate with that in mind.

[146:59]We're gonna have this, you know, you go into a debate not to persuade the people that are

[147:03]already supporting you, but to persuade the people that are not.

[147:06]And so it was very important to me going into the debate to reframe the issue from an analytic

[147:13]perspective because

[147:15]The reason that so many people supported the strikes on Iran is because they are guzzling

[147:20]the propaganda about Iran and about the conflict.

[147:23]In the mind of your average conservative, they believe that Iran is chanting death to

[147:29]America.

[147:30]We're the great Satan, Israel's little Satan, and Iran is racing towards a nuclear bomb

[147:36]like they always have been.

[147:41]And the argument goes, why not take a chance and bomb their nuclear program?

[147:48]The argument went, we're not invading.

[147:51]We're not even necessarily seeking regime change or trying to affect it ourselves directly.

[147:57]But we're going to bomb them now that Israel has cleared the runway and then we're going

[148:01]to go home and we're going to say mission accomplished and it's America first because

[148:06]we're not neocons, but we're not isolationists.

[148:09]And that was sort of the argument.

[148:10]And so I went into it with the goal of reframing it from our perspective, from the realist

[148:16]perspective, which is to say, let's go back to the beginning of the story.

[148:22]Why does Iran want a nuclear bomb?

[148:24]And what is the status of their nuclear program?

[148:27]I said, well, it's instructive to understand

[148:29]that this is a real strategy.

[148:31]It's called hedging.

[148:32]They have the ability to make one

[148:34]and yet they haven't made one.

[148:36]Why?

[148:37]Well, the reason they have the ability to make one

[148:40]but haven't made one yet

[148:41]is because they're hedging their bets

[148:44]with their security situation in mind.

[148:46]They fear regime change

[148:52]And because we've done it to all of their neighbors in the past 30 years, they know

[148:57]that if they don't have a deterrent, they're next.

[149:00]But if they acquire a nuclear bomb, they're going to be next anyway.

[149:04]That will invite the United States to intervene.

[149:07]I said so from an analytic perspective, this is how we have to approach the subject matter

[149:17]And what is the strategy?

[149:19]What is the calculation behind why things are the way that they are?

[149:24]Because once we begin to analyze this, then we can begin to understand what America's role should be in the conflict.

[149:31]And that's really where I started from, and as predicted, and I credit to Dinesh for being intellectually honest on some level.

[149:41]He presented the argument that he's always presented

[149:43]for 20 years, which is that Iran is an Islamist theocracy.

[149:48]They are caliphate.

[149:49]They want to spread Islam at any cost.

[149:51]They want to end the world.

[149:52]We must take them at their word

[149:54]when they say death to America.

[149:55]And that's why we should intervene.

[149:57]And he said he's not in favor of boots on the ground

[150:00]and this sort of thing.

[150:02]He said, but we should take action against Iran

[150:05]to make our country safe.

[150:08]And so the debate went, I think about how you would expect.

[150:11]I think I answered and sort of unraveled a lot of these assumptions that were made.

[150:16]For example, the death to America thing.

[150:19]They will always cite one or another cleric in the Iranian government, Iranian society,

[150:26]and say, oh, well, you know, so and so said, we're going to destroy America.

[150:30]And I said, well, the top of the Iranian government that had clerics said they're not going to

[150:36]I said, so what are we to believe?

[150:38]If we're going to take them at their word, do we believe one random cleric or do we believe

[150:42]the supreme leader?

[150:43]And he said, I believe I ran over you.

[150:46]I said, well, well, good.

[150:47]If we're believing I ran, then let's see what they've said about nukes.

[150:51]They say they don't have one.

[150:53]They don't want one.

[150:53]And as a matter of fact, it's against Islamic law.

[150:56]It's immoral to acquire one.

[150:58]And people say, well, but later you talk about how Iran wants a nuclear weapon.

[151:04]Now, the reason you spell that out is to say we can't just look at what they say.

[151:09]To go out and make this argument that we have to bomb Iran because they say death to America

[151:15]and that's good enough evidence, we're going to take them at their word.

[151:19]Our cause of war is they said death to America.

[151:27]It's part of a foreign policy.

[151:29]What the Supreme Leader says, what the Foreign Minister says, what members of the Iranian

[151:35]Parliament say, these words they say, they're not the end all be all.

[151:40]You have to look.

[151:41]at the decision-making that underlies those words.

[151:44]You have to look at the calculation

[151:45]and the strategy that underlies them

[151:48]because words are words and words can be rhetoric

[151:51]and words can be used in the context of foreign policy.

[151:54]We have to go deeper into their foreign policy.

[151:56]I said, once we begin to unpack the history of the region,

[152:00]then it's very clear what's going on here,

[152:03]which is we're trapped in this three-way paradigm.

[152:07]where Israel wants regime change to make themselves safe.

[152:11]Iran wants a nuclear weapon to make themselves safe against Israel.

[152:20]or rather, we don't want them to have a nuke

[152:22]to make our world safe because we're the hegemon of the world.

[152:33]but I said, once we unpack that,

[152:36]that's the fundamental undercurrent

[152:38]that is driving all of these events,

[152:41]then we can begin to understand relationally

[152:45]what our role is and what we should do here.

[152:49]we can see that our foreign policy,

[152:52]our objective is distinct from Israel's.

[153:34]was, well, that's a Democrat way of looking at it.

[174:38]This is the background on it.

[174:40]When Trump won the election in 2024, he quickly got together with congressional leaders and

[174:46]said that he wanted, and this is where the name comes from, as opposed to having multiple

[174:51]bills that deal with different aspects of his agenda.

[174:55]He said, and this is very contentious,

[174:57]he said he wanted one big, beautiful bill

[174:59]that includes everything.

[175:01]In the early stages of the transition,

[175:04]and then in the early days after the inauguration,

[175:07]there was a debate between the House and the Senate

[175:10]on whether they'd have one bill

[175:13]and one bill that would deal with the debt ceiling,

[175:16]which is looming later in the year,

[175:18]and another bill which would deal with everything else.

[175:23]There was this contentious debate between the two chambers and the White House,

[175:27]are we going to have multiple bills or one bill? And the way that Trump marketed his-

[175:34]preference. He said, I want one bill, one big beautiful bill. And this is how it came

[175:40]to be known as the One Big Beautiful Bill Act. And what he wants in there is all of the big

[175:47]legislation that he promised during the campaign. Among those things are the corporate tax cut.

[175:53]The signature piece of legislation from Trump's first term, the 2016 Tax and Jobs Act, it

[176:16]a significant reduction in the corporate tax.

[176:23]There was a lot of expensing for businesses.

[176:25]There was a lot more

[176:30]for people to take the standard deduction

[176:38]of this one big, beautiful bill

[176:39]is extending those tax cuts,

[176:42]which are set to expire at the end of this year.

[176:45]So, unless this bill passes with that extension in there, all the tax cuts expire and it becomes

[176:53]effectively a tax hike.

[176:56]It's a major tax hike on corporations.

[176:59]It's gonna be a tax hike more or less for income earners.

[177:03]And it's also going to affect investment because, again, there won't be those expensive

[177:07]benefits.

[177:08]So, that is a huge part of it.

[177:15]The other aspect of it are all these other tax advantages that Trump promised, no tax

[177:20]on tips, no tax on interest on car payments, no tax on social security benefits.

[177:28]Those are the others, and there's others too, but those are the big pieces that are also

[177:32]part of the tax agenda that is in there.

[177:35]Then the next biggest thing is in order for Trump to deliver on a border wall and mass deportations

[177:41]There needs to be a lot more money for customs and border patrol as well as for ICE and

[177:49]That means money for a border wall and for enforcement of the southern border, but also interior enforcement

[177:56]That is deportations. So what this bill also does is it allocates a hundred and fifty billion dollars?

[178:04]which is huge over four years for building a border wall,

[178:09]$46 billion for a wall and about $20 or $30 billion

[178:13]for border patrol agents, technology at the border,

[178:17]infrastructure at the border like detention centers,

[178:20]but also there's about $80 billion for interior enforcement.

[178:24]That means 10,000 more ICE agents,

[178:27]100,000 detention beds and just money

[178:31]for the department in general.

[178:33]The biggest problem with the border, it was almost engineered this way is there just are

[178:40]not the resources to carry out a mass deportation.

[178:44]Why?

[178:45]Well, one, we don't have enough agents.

[178:48]What they're finding out implementing deportations is that ICE agents have never deported people.

[178:54]Many of the ICE agents that work for ICE under DHS,

[178:59]they don't deport people.

[179:00]They literally do not have the expertise.

[179:02]This is something they're finding out.

[179:04]This is a real problem.

[179:06]It is a real struggle right now

[179:07]that they have had to rapidly train up ICE

[179:11]because this is not like a deportation force.

[179:14]The other big problem is they can't detain all these people.

[179:19]ICE will go and make arrests.

[179:21]And then while you're adjudicating whether they can stay in the country or not,

[179:25]you have to put them in a jail.

[179:26]You gotta detain them somewhere.

[179:29]And we have like 40,000 beds across the whole country.

[179:33]Well, if you wanted to port a million people per year,

[179:35]you're gonna need more than this rotating set of 40,000 beds.

[179:39]So they want to have, they want to more than double the amount of beds in the country or

[179:43]add more than double the amount on top of what we already have.

[179:47]And then the border wall is self-explanatory.

[179:50]Now just to put this in a perspective about the border money, back in 2017, when Trump

[179:57]won the first time, he was putting together his proposal for the border wall back then.

[180:06]It cost 17 billion.

[180:10]So in January, 2018, a year after Trump was inaugurated the first time, they put together

[180:16]their blueprint for their border wall, 30 foot concrete wall, eventually turned into

[180:21]an 18 foot steel ballard wall with a climbing plate.

[180:24]They said 17 billion is what we need and we need 6 billion for border patrol.

[180:34]Altogether was 23 billion and the Republicans then that controlled the House and the Senate said no

[180:42]They said we're not giving you 23 billion. We're not giving you 17. You know what the compromise was?

[180:49]1.8 billion dollars

[180:52]By the time they passed the omnibus bill later in the spring of 2018 they allocated 1.8 billion

[181:01]for fencing along the Rio Grande Valley region.

[181:05]And that's it.

[181:06]And then the same thing happened again,

[181:08]after the midterms in November, 2018,

[181:11]during the lame duck period,

[181:12]Trump tried to get money for the border wall.

[181:14]You know what Congress agreed to?

[181:16]$1.6 billion.

[181:20]So, in the first term, $23 billion was way too much.

[181:24]They said best we could do is $3 billion for a border wall.

[181:28]In this bill, $150 billion.

[181:33]It's literally 100 times more than what Republicans would give us in the first term.

[181:40]And initially, I thought it was too good to be true.

[181:42]I called the experts.

[181:45]I know many of the people that are involved in the process.

[181:48]I called the experts and I said,

[181:50]what's going on with this border bill?

[181:52]I said, it seems too good to be true.

[181:55]10,000 ICE agents, 46 billion for a wall,

[181:59]100,000 detention beds.

[182:00]I said, it just, what is with the 180 here?

[182:04]I said, is it really this good?

[182:07]And they said, yeah.

[182:08]I said, why?

[182:10]I said, is it because of pressure from the base?

[182:12]Is it because immigration just got that bad?

[182:14]They said, well, it's both.

[182:16]And it's true.

[182:17]Over four years, they will disperse $150 billion.

[182:21]But there's another catch and we'll get into that in a moment.

[182:23]But that's part of it too.

[182:26]Now in order to secure concessions on all these things, there's been a number of other

[182:32]Deals that have been made concerning clean energy tax credits, salt deductions, in order

[182:38]to pay for the corporate tax cuts, they're making major cuts to Medicaid.

[182:43]Now that's the bill.

[182:46]They want to put all of it together.

[182:47]And the reason why is because Trump is not going to let his agenda get deferred.

[182:52]I think Trump knows, and I think even the MAGA Republicans are smart enough to understand,

[182:59]that what happened in the first term was denial by delay, which is to say, Trump came in in

[183:07]the first term wanting to close the border with the border wall.

[183:11]Now Republicans didn't say, you can't have your border wall.

[183:15]What they said was, we'll do it later.

[183:22]When Trump got in in 17, the Republican conference came together to make their agenda and they

[183:27]said, our first priority is repealing Obamacare.

[183:30]Our second priority is cutting taxes.

[183:33]They said, then once we've done that, we'll win the midterms.

[183:38]After the midterms, then you'll get your border wall.

[183:42]Well, guess what?

[183:43]They ran out the clock.

[183:45]They in 2017 tried and failed to repeal Obamacare in 2018. They got the tax and jobs act through

[183:53]Then they lost the house in the midterms and then the dream of the border wall at least in Congress was over and they said oops

[184:00]Oh

[184:01]Too bad guess we're never gonna get a border wall

[184:04]They said, you know, look we tried we gave you a few billion dollars, but you know political realities and such

[184:10]We had our plan. Oops, it didn't work. We lost the house. I guess we got our corporate tax cut

[184:15]anyway. This is how they played in the first term. Now, in 2025, it was deja vu all over again,

[184:22]because what Republicans do, they came in and they said, we want to extend the tax cut from 2017.

[184:30]They said our priority is extending the corporate tax cut for the second term.

[184:39]If you want an extension of the corporate tax cut,

[184:41]then we're getting money for my border wall.

[184:44]I won the second election, I took a bullet,

[184:46]they were trying to throw me in jail.

[184:49]We're not splitting, because you know what happened

[184:51]if you split them up, we'd get one and not the other.

[184:53]That's why it's one big, beautiful bill.

[184:56]Because what the Senate wanted to do was,

[184:59]we'll have one bill for the tax cuts

[185:01]and a bunch of bills for everything else.

[185:03]And you know what that means.

[185:04]It means we'll get the tax cut and we'll get the border never, okay?

[185:09]We'll get that on October 32nd, like it's never gonna happen.

[185:13]So Trump said, if we're getting the corporate tax cut, we're getting the border money too.

[185:17]This is created, I think, at the center of it, I think this is really the issue.

[185:21]There's ancillary stuff.

[185:23]There's debates about protecting the supply chains for solar and whether the tax credits

[185:29]are incentivizing buying from China and therefore subsidizing their industry.

[185:34]There's arguments about salt deductions.

[185:37]The Republicans in New York have been lobbying for years.

[185:40]They want a bigger salt deduction.

[185:42]They want their constituents to deduct their state and local tax.

[185:46]And there's some horse trading going on.

[185:50]And a big thing is they want to raise the debt ceiling by $4 trillion or $5 trillion and that's got fiscal conservatives angry because we're getting to a big crunch fiscally coming up very soon.

[186:02]But I think at the center of it is a lot of the Republicans, I think, don't want the immigration aspect of it.

[186:09]Now, the reason that they have to do it in this manner, a lot of people, like I said earlier,

[186:14]People are saying, well, if you have this issue,

[186:19]why don't you split up the bills?

[186:21]Ignoring the political reality,

[186:23]they say, why don't you split up the bills?

[186:25]And we'll vote on the least contentious thing,

[186:28]which is the border money,

[186:29]separately from the most contentious things,

[186:32]like the debt ceiling, like salt,

[186:34]like the energy credits.

[186:37]The reason they have to package it into one

[186:39]is because of how they're passing it.

[186:42]They're passing it with a process called budget reconciliation.

[186:46]Like I said earlier, it gets a little technical here, but the rules of the Senate are such

[186:51]that in order to get any major legislation passed, any major political appointee confirmed

[186:57]in the Senate, you need 60 out of 100 votes.

[187:01]That's a super majority, not a simple majority.

[187:04]And that has to do with the rule concerning the filibuster.

[187:10]on any item, like a confirmation, like legislation,

[187:15]you need to invoke cloture to end debate,

[187:18]to end a filibuster.

[187:19]To do that, you need 60 votes.

[187:21]There is one exception to this every year.

[187:25]This is a process called budget reconciliation.

[187:28]It came about in the 70s.

[187:30]And they said that once per year,

[187:33]technically three times, but realistically once,

[187:36]you can pass one bill.

[187:39]It must be related to budget, must be related to budget outlays, it is not having anything

[187:45]to do with policy.

[187:47]Once per fiscal year, you can pass a bill that either increases revenue, decreases spending,

[187:54]or increases the debt ceiling, it can do one or all of those things, and you only need

[188:01]a simple majority to pass it, only 51 votes.

[188:08]So this is the only way we're gonna be able to pass anything is with budget reconciliation.

[188:13]We don't have, we're never gonna get seven Democrats to vote for money for a border wall.

[188:18]We're never gonna get seven Democrats to vote on anything.

[188:21]So because we only have 53, technically 54 votes in the Senate.

[188:27]We must use budget reconciliation

[188:29]to get any major policy through.

[188:31]And we could do it twice.

[188:33]We could do it in the fiscal year for 2025

[188:35]and the fiscal year for 2026, and then that's it.

[188:39]Now, this bill, that's why there's so much debate

[188:42]about the bill, because the corporate tax cut

[188:46]is gonna cost money.

[188:47]If you cut taxes, you bring in less revenue,

[188:50]which means,

[188:51]We're spending more money.

[188:53]We're bringing less revenue, the deficit goes up.

[188:56]So in order to unlock the budget reconciliation process,

[189:01]it must be at the minimum revenue neutral,

[189:03]so we have to match the loss in revenue with spending cuts.

[189:08]You wanna cut corporate taxes,

[189:10]which means reducing revenue in the long term.

[189:14]You wanna increase spending on the border,

[189:16]which means more spending.

[189:17]You have to pay for those things.

[189:19]You have to make up for the lost revenue

[189:21]and the increase in spending on a number of things

[189:24]by cutting spending elsewhere.

[189:27]And they're looking around,

[189:28]that's a big part of what Doge was about actually,

[189:31]that's a part of it.

[189:33]They're looking around, where can we make cuts?

[189:36]Well, they don't want to cut the military because we're at war with everybody.

[189:40]They don't want to cut social security because you can't touch social security.

[189:44]You'll never win an election again.

[189:45]You can't touch Medicare because you piss off the retirees, forget about the midterms.

[189:51]Well, the biggest entitlement after social security and Medicare, got to go for Medicaid,

[189:57]which is healthcare for the poor.

[189:59]So they're making these deep cuts to Medicaid, like $900 billion over a number of years.

[190:07]And this is very controversial because this is not really a good look.

[190:12]We're cutting taxes for corporations, which is going to cost money.

[190:17]We're paying for that by getting rid of people's health care.

[190:21]Now, you can make arguments about it.

[190:23]If you look at what they're doing, it's not extreme.

[190:25]They're really attaching work requirements to Medicaid.

[190:28]They're saying to get your free health care,

[190:31]you need to get a job.

[190:33]That's not crazy.

[190:34]It's not undoable.

[190:36]But it's not good optics because the optics of it is you're taking people. It's very sensitive. It's a sensitive issue

[190:42]You're taking health care from people to give tax breaks to corporations

[190:48]It's kind of like the Republicans worst nightmare and the bill is extremely unpopular for this reason

[190:56]And the Democrats are going all in on this.

[190:59]They're cutting Medicaid.

[191:01]They're taking healthcare to pay for corporate taxes.

[191:06]which is that at the same time,

[191:08]Republicans promised they'd cut the deficit

[191:11]and this is gonna increase the deficit

[191:13]more than anything in history.

[191:15]Republicans were saying they would balance the budget,

[191:18]actually, they would eliminate the deficit.

[191:21]They would reduce the deficit by $2 trillion.

[191:24]And now, not only are we not reducing it at all, it's not even staying the same, it's

[191:29]going up by trillions of dollars historically.

[191:31]And the debt ceiling's going up $4 trillion.

[191:34]We're going to have a $40 trillion debt ceiling with multi-trillion dollar deficits for years.

[191:41]So, that's a huge broken promise, and Elon is furious.

[191:44]Elon donated nearly $300 million to the Trump campaign.

[191:59]Increases it by trillions.

[192:02]Elon complains, he says, you broke your promise, and Trump says, well, I'm going to deport

[192:08]You better watch what you say, because I'm going to bring the government down on you

[192:25]It might.

[192:26]I think it won't have a major impact.

[192:32]The other issue is that there are fiscal hawks inside the GOP, like Rand Paul, like Thomas

[192:38]Massey, like members of the Freedom Caucus.

[192:41]And because there's such a slim majority in the House of only three votes, the Republicans

[192:47]need everybody on board to vote for it.

[192:49]And if you have a handful of them that say we won't go with an increase in the debt ceiling,

[192:54]we won't go with more reckless spending, it's gonna grind the whole thing to a halt.

[192:59]And so this is the extremely complicated situation that we find ourselves in.

[194:51]you need to match that dollar for dollar with further cuts.

[270:06]You only need one missile to get through, whereas interceptors need to be 100% successful.

[270:15]Interceptors are expensive and need many interceptors for one missile.

[270:22]to hit its target than for an interceptor
